name: Gull Point
short_description: Scenic Keweenaw Peninsula rockhounding site on Lake Superior shoreline offering access to Precambrian volcanic agates and chalcedony specimens in wave-tumbled beach deposits.
description: Gull Point is a rocky coastal area along Lake Superior's shoreline in Michigan's remote Keweenaw Peninsula, known for its abundant beach deposits of Lake Superior agates and other silica-based minerals. The site sits at the intersection of waves and ancient volcanic bedrock, where Precambrian lava flows have weathered and eroded into the lake for millions of years, creating excellent collecting opportunities along the shoreline. Rockhounders here search the beach gravels and cobbles for banded agates, chalcedony nodules, and jasper fragments that range from thumb-sized to fist-sized specimens. The flat terrain and easy shoreline access make this an excellent location for families and casual collectors, though the remote location requires careful planning. The combination of rich geology, scenic lake views, and productive collecting makes Gull Point a destination worth the drive for serious agate hunters.
history: This area lies within the Keweenaw Peninsula's Precambrian volcanic formation, part of the 1.1 billion-year-old Midcontinent Rift System. Ancient flood basalts and felsic volcanic flows created silica-rich bedrock that has been naturally processed by Lake Superior's wave action for millennia, concentrating agates and other cryptocrystalline minerals in the beach deposits that collectors find today.
tips: Walk the beach near the waterline and in the shallow wave-worn gravel bars where heavier stones accumulate - this is where the best agates concentrate. Look for translucent to opaque stones with banding or chatoyance; Lake Superior agates often have distinctive orange, red, or brown coloring. Bring a small 5-gallon bucket to carry specimens, a magnifying glass to inspect fine details, and wear sturdy shoes with good traction on wet rocks. Early morning or after storms can yield fresh material as waves churn up new deposits overnight.
terrain_notes: Flat elevation at 602 feet with minimal slope. Terrain consists of rocky Lake Superior shoreline with beach gravel, cobbles, and bedrock outcrops. Walking surface is uneven and frequently wet from wave action and seepage. No established parking lot; access via informal pullouts. Approximately 0.25 miles walking distance from parking to productive collecting areas. Footing is slippery and requires careful stepping on wet rocks.
best_season: May - September. Summer and early fall offer the most pleasant conditions for extended beach collecting, though high water levels in early summer can limit access to some gravel bars. Spring and fall can produce excellent finds as thermal cycling loosens specimens, but cold water and shorter daylight limit comfort.
written_directions: From Ontonagon, take US-45 north about 18 miles toward the Keweenaw Peninsula. Continue on local roads following signs toward Carp Lake Township and the Lake Superior shoreline. GPS coordinates (46.836129, -89.5495) direct you to informal roadside pullouts near Gull Point. The final approach involves unpaved forest service roads with moderate washboard. High-clearance or AWD vehicles recommended, especially in wet seasons. Park in designated pull-offs and walk to the shoreline.
road_conditions: Unpaved forest service roads with washboard surface. High clearance recommended. May be muddy or rutted in wet seasons. Passenger cars can access with careful driving in dry conditions.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along forest service roads near the shoreline. No developed parking lot. Space for 3-5 vehicles. No facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or power tools. Collect from beach surface and loose deposits only - do not remove material from bedrock outcrops. Leave all archaeological and paleontological materials undisturbed. Respect private property boundaries if marked.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No power tools
Surface collection from beach and loose deposits only
Do not extract from bedrock
Respect marked private property
nearest_services: Ontonagon, approximately 20 miles south, offers gas, food, and lodging. Smaller stores in White Pine area. No services at the site itself.
accessibility_notes: Flat terrain but very uneven rocky shoreline with wet, slippery footing. No wheelchair access to productive collecting areas. Sturdy waterproof boots with good traction strongly recommended. Not suitable for walkers or limited mobility. Wave action and seepage create constantly changing conditions.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding at Gull Point Michigan?
faq.answer: Gull Point is known for Lake Superior agates, chalcedony, jasper, and quartz specimens found in beach gravels. The agates often display distinctive banding and range from thumbnail to fist-sized. These Precambrian minerals have been naturally polished by Lake Superior wave action for millions of years.

faq.question: Is there a fee to rockhound at Gull Point on Lake Superior?
faq.answer: No fee is required to rockhound at Gull Point. The site is on National Forest land and allows free personal use collection up to 25 pounds per day. No permits are needed for casual agate hunting at this location.

faq.question: How do you find Lake Superior agates at Gull Point?
faq.answer: Search the beach at waterline level and in wave-worn gravel bars where heavier stones accumulate. Look for translucent or banded stones with orange, red, or brown coloring. Use a magnifying glass to spot the waxy luster characteristic of Lake Superior agates. Early morning and post-storm collecting often yields fresh material.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Gull Point Michigan?
faq.answer: May through September offers the most comfortable collecting conditions with pleasant weather and longer daylight hours. Spring and fall can produce excellent finds due to thermal cycling that loosens specimens, though water temperatures remain cold and access may be limited by high water levels.

faq.question: Can families with kids rockhound at Gull Point?
faq.answer: Yes, Gull Point is family-friendly for children ages 6 and up. The flat terrain and easy shoreline access make it accessible for families. However, wet rocky footing requires supervision and sturdy footwear. The remote location means you should bring supplies and plan for a full day away from services.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need for rockhounding near Ontonagon Michigan?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy waterproof boots with good traction, a 5-gallon bucket for specimens, and a magnifying glass for examining finds. A UV light can help spot agates in beach gravel. The wet shoreline conditions make slip-resistant shoes essential. Bring water, sunscreen, and insect repellent for this remote location.

meta_title: Gull Point Rockhounding - Michigan Lake Superior
meta_description: Find Lake Superior agates, chalcedony, and jasper at Gull Point Michigan. Free public rockhounding on scenic shoreline near Ontonagon. Best May-September.
